summary: Adversarial bug audit of FileID Windows Rust engine (safety/concurrency/DB/ML)
logs: Engine audit: 7 finder dimensions, refute-by-default verify
Engine audit done: 4 confirmed, 0 uncertain, 0 refuted (of 4 raw)

confirmed.title: wipeLibrary does not interlock against an in-flight face-clustering run, leaving ghost persons after a wipe
confirmed.description:
The face-clustering refactor split the handler into read(lock) -> compute(LOCK-FREE, multi-second) -> write(lock) (commands/face_clustering.rs: PHASE 1 reads under db.lock() and drops it at line 171; PHASE 2 clusters with NO lock held for seconds; PHASE 3 re-acquires db.lock() at line 288 and persists). The persist transaction is: `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id = NULL` (line 292), `DELETE FROM persons` (line 293), then re-INSERT one persons row per anchor computed in PHASE 2 (lines 338-355), then `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id = ?` (lines 360-364).

`handle_wipe_library` was written to interlock only against a running SCAN: it cancels the scan coordinator and waits (bounded, ~5 s) for `scan_state` to become None before wiping (wipe.rs lines 27-34). It has NO knowledge of the new `face_cluster_active` single-flight flag (declared in main.rs:308 and threaded to the dispatcher) and does not wait for a clustering run to finish. Both operations correctly serialize on the writer mutex (no DB corruption), but the *ordering* is unguarded.

Concrete reachable sequence: a scan completes -> the app auto-fires `runFaceClustering` (the handler's own comment at face_clustering.rs:122-124 states it is auto-fired after every scan completion) -> clustering enters PHASE 2 (lock-free, holding only `face_cluster_active=true`, holding NO db lock) -> the user, seeing the scan finished, clicks 'Wipe Library'. Wipe's scan interlock returns immediately (scan_state is already None), wipe acquires db.lock(), empties `persons` + `face_prints` + everything, commits, VACUUMs, emits `LibraryWiped{ok:true}`. Clustering's PHASE 3 then acquires the now-free db.lock() and runs its persist against the wiped DB: the NULL-update and DELETE touch 0 rows, but the per-anchor `INSERT INTO persons` (anchors built from the pre-wipe faces in PHASE 2) re-creates phantom person rows whose `representative_face_id` points at face ids that no longer exist. `persons.representative_face_id` has only an index, no FK (db/migrations.rs:200), so the INSERT succeeds. The final `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id` matches 0 rows.
confirmed.impact: After a 'wipe library' that reports success, the People tab is repopulated with ghost person clusters that reference deleted/non-existent faces (broken thumbnails, zero real members). These phantoms persist until the next scan+cluster cycle (a wipe emits LibraryWiped, not scanComplete, so no auto-cluster fires to clean them up). The user wiped their library but the People tab still shows people — directly contradicting the wipe confirmation, the exact failure class the existing scan interlock was added to prevent (see wipe.rs comment lines 20-26).
confirmed.fix: Extend the wipe interlock to also wait for face clustering, mirroring the scan_state wait. Thread the `face_cluster_active: Arc<AtomicBool>` into `handle_wipe_library` (it is already available at the WipeLibrary dispatch site in main.rs:807-818 as a handle_line parameter), and before the spawn_blocking wipe, busy-wait (bounded, e.g. up to ~5 s at 50 ms) while `face_cluster_active.load(Acquire)` is true. Clustering's RAII `FaceClusterActiveGuard` clears the flag on PHASE 3 completion/error/panic, so the wait terminates. This guarantees the clustering persist either fully precedes or is absent when the wipe runs, so no anchors are written into a wiped DB. (Per the engine's single-DB-owner principle, enforce this here regardless of app-side ordering.)
confirmed.evidence: wipe.rs:27-34 waits only on scan_state; face_clustering.rs:171 drops the lock, :202 clusters lock-free, :288 re-acquires, :293 DELETE FROM persons, :338-355 re-INSERT persons from PHASE-2 anchors; main.rs:308 declares face_cluster_active but wipe never receives it; db/migrations.rs:200 shows representative_face_id has no FK so the orphan INSERT succeeds.

verdict.reasoning:
I independently traced every load-bearing claim and they all hold.

1. wipe.rs:15-34 — handle_wipe_library receives only (sink, db, scan_state). Its interlock (lines 27-34) cancels the scan coordinator and busy-waits (bounded: 100 x 50ms = 5s, line 31) for scan_state to become None. It has zero awareness of face_cluster_active and does not wait for a clustering run.

2. main.rs dispatch — face_cluster_active IS declared (line 308) and IS available at the WipeLibrary dispatch site, but the WipeLibrary arm (main.rs:807-818) threads only db + scan_state into handle_wipe_library; it never passes face_cluster_active. The RunFaceClustering arm (779-805) takes the single-flight via compare_exchange and holds a FaceClusterActiveGuard for the spawned task.

3. face_clustering.rs three-phase split confirmed: PHASE 1 reads under db.lock() and drops the conn at line 171; PHASE 2 (174-282) runs cluster()+consolidate() with NO lock held (genuinely multi-second on the project's tens-of-thousands-of-faces scale); PHASE 3 re-acquires db.lock() at line 288, then UPDATE face_prints SET person_id=NULL (292), DELETE FROM persons (293), per-anchor INSERT INTO persons (338-355), UPDATE face_prints SET person_id (360-364). Critically, PHASE 3 reads NOTHING new from the DB — anchors/assignments are entirely PHASE-2 in-memory state — so the per-anchor INSERTs run unconditionally even against a freshly wiped DB.

4. migrations.rs:185-200 — persons.representative_face_id has only idx_person_repface (line 200), NO foreign key. The orphan INSERT (representative_face_id pointing at a deleted face id) succeeds.

5. Reachability (app side) confirmed: EngineClient.cs:1062 auto-fires AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync on every ScanCompleteEvent; the wipe button (SidebarFolderHeader.xaml.cs) has a re-entrancy guard (_wipeInFlight) that only blocks double-WIPE clicks — there is NO gate against clustering being in-flight. RunWipeAsync calls WipeLibraryAndWaitAsync against the running engine.

Concrete reachable sequence: scan completes -> app auto-fires runFaceClustering -> clustering enters PHASE 2 (lock-free, face_cluster_active=true, no db lock) -> user clicks Wipe -> wipe's scan interlock returns immediately (scan_state already None) -> wipe acquires db.lock(), wipe_all() DELETEs persons + face_prints in one tx (db/mod.rs:142-187), commits, emits LibraryWiped{ok:true} -> clustering PHASE 3 acquires the now-free lock, its NULL-update + DELETE touch 0 rows, but per-anchor INSERT INTO persons re-creates phantom person rows referencing now-deleted face ids; the final face_prints UPDATE matches 0 rows. Both ops serialize on the writer mutex (no corruption) but the ORDER is unguarded.

User-visible impact: after a wipe that reports success, the People tab is repopulated with ghost clusters that reference deleted faces (broken thumbnails, zero real members). A wipe emits LibraryWiped, not ScanComplete, so no auto-cluster fires to clean them up; the phantoms persist until the next scan+cluster. This directly contradicts the wipe confirmation — the exact failure class the existing scan interlock (wipe.rs comment 20-26) was added to prevent, and a violation of the engine's stated single-DB-owner / self-enforce principle.

I downgrade severity from high to medium: the bug is real and reachable but requires a narrow user-timed race (the user must click Wipe during the lock-free PHASE 2 window right after a scan), not a deterministic failure. It is data-cosmetic (ghost rows, not corruption) and self-heals on the next scan+cluster.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct in approach and complete enough to land: thread face_cluster_active: Arc<AtomicBool> into handle_wipe_library (it's available at the dispatch site), and before the spawn_blocking wipe, busy-wait (bounded, mirroring the existing scan wait) while face_cluster_active.load(Acquire) is true. FaceClusterActiveGuard (main.rs:439-444) clears the flag on Drop for every PHASE-3 exit path (completion/error/panic via Release store), so the wait terminates. One nuance the finding understates: the bounded ~5s wait does not FULLY close the window — if PHASE 2 runs longer than the wait bound (large corpus is exactly when the lock-free window is widest), the wait times out and the wipe proceeds, re-opening the race. This is acceptable as a best-effort match to the existing scan interlock, but for a hard guarantee the wait should also actively pre-empt clustering (e.g. a cancel flag clustering checks before its PHASE-3 persist) rather than only waiting. As a minimal, codebase-consistent fix the proposed busy-wait is the right call; I add the cancel-flag note as the robust upgrade.

confirmed.title: Single-file Deep Analyze reports a genuine per-file failure as cancelled:true, mislabeling the UI and suppressing the app's failure warning
confirmed.description:
In the single-file Deep Analyze handler, the `Err(err)` arm (when `analyze_file(...)` fails for a real per-file reason — source file missing, unsupported/HEIC decode, webp transcode failure, VLM subprocess crash, PDF render error, persist failure) emits the terminal event `DeepAnalyzeComplete { processed: 0, failed: 1, cancelled: true }`. The `cancelled: true` is wrong: the run was not cancelled, it failed. The comment on lines 159-160 claims it is 'mirroring the batch handler's convention', but the batch handler (`run_deep_analyze_batch`, line 563) emits `cancelled: false` on the normal-completion path and only sets `cancelled: true` on an actual cancel (line 412) — so the single-file arm diverges from the very convention it cites.

The mislabel is consumed two ways on the app side. (1) `DeepAnalyzeView.SyncStream` (Views/DeepAnalyze/DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:432) keys its terminal text on `complete.Cancelled`, so the user sees 'Cancelled (0 done, 1 failed)' for a file that actually failed — the UI tells the user they cancelled something they did not. (2) `EngineClient.Commands.cs:431` gates its dedicated warning toast on `if (!result.Cancelled && result.Failed > 0)`; because `Cancelled` is true, the actionable 'Deep Analyze couldn't process this file. It may be an unsupported format, or the model isn't installed yet.' warning is suppressed. A separate `Error` event is still emitted (line 152), so the spinner is not stranded, but the specific failure guidance the app authored for exactly this case never reaches the user, and the result line lies about the cause.
confirmed.evidence: deep_analyze.rs:161-169 emits DeepAnalyzeComplete{failed:1, cancelled:true} in the analyze-failure Err arm. Batch path (deep_analyze.rs:557-565) emits cancelled:false on the same kind of failure (failed counted, cancelled false). App: EngineClient.Commands.cs:431 `if (!result.Cancelled && result.Failed > 0)` suppresses LastWarning when Cancelled; DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:432 renders 'Cancelled (...)' when complete.Cancelled is true.
confirmed.impact: A real single-file Deep Analyze failure (e.g. HEIC/unsupported image, missing source file, VLM crash) is presented to the user as 'Cancelled' and the app's dedicated 'couldn't process this file / unsupported format' warning toast is silently suppressed by the `!result.Cancelled` guard. The user gets a misleading result line and loses the actionable hint, making a genuinely failed file look like a user cancellation.
confirmed.fix: In the analyze-failure Err arm of `handle_deep_analyze_file`, set `cancelled: false` (line 167) so the terminal event reflects a failure, not a cancel — matching the batch handler. With `cancelled:false, failed:1` the app's warning at EngineClient.Commands.cs:431 fires and DeepAnalyzeView shows 'Done — 0 captioned ... (1 failed)'. (Optionally, distinguish a cancel-induced Err by checking `cancel.load(Relaxed)` and only then set cancelled:true, mirroring how the batch loop reads the flag.) The no-runner early-return arm at lines 60-69 is defensible as-is since it pairs with a llama_cpp_missing Error and the suppressed duplicate warning is intended, but the genuine-analyze-failure arm should not be cancelled:true.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all three claims against the actual code.

ENGINE (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/deep_analyze.rs):
- `handle_deep_analyze_file` (line 33) calls `analyze_file(...)` (line 86). Its failure arm `Err(err)` (lines 151-172) emits an `Error` event (line 152) AND a terminal `DeepAnalyzeComplete { processed: 0, failed: 1, cancelled: true }` — `cancelled: true` is hard-coded at line 167, unconditionally, for ANY analyze error.
- This is genuinely reachable for non-cancellation failures. In platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/deep_analyze.rs, `analyze_file` returns Err for: missing VLM weights (line 122), source file missing (line 227), unsupported kind (line 260), image open/decode/encode failures incl. HEIC/webp transcode (lines 277-295), PDF render failures (lines 494-502). These are real per-file failures, NOT cancellation. Only the `anyhow::bail!("cancelled")` sites (lines 135/156/171/401/...) are genuine cancels, yet the handler labels them all `cancelled: true`.
- The comment at lines 159-160 claims it is "mirroring the batch handler's convention", but the batch handler `run_deep_analyze_batch` does the opposite: a per-file failure increments `failed` and continues (lines 550-553), and the normal-completion terminal event emits `cancelled: false` (lines 557-565). So the single-file arm contradicts the very convention it cites. (Batch only sets `cancelled: true` on a real cancel at line 412, where it reads `cancel.load(Ordering::Relaxed)` at line 405.)

APP (consumes the mislabel two ways, both confirmed):
1. ViewModels/EngineClient.Commands.cs:431 — `if (!result.Cancelled && result.Failed > 0)`. With `Cancelled:true, Failed:1` this is `(!true && true) = false`, so the dedicated `LastWarning` toast ("Deep Analyze couldn't process this file. It may be an unsupported format, or the model isn't installed yet.") at lines 433-437 is silently suppressed for a genuine failure.
2. Views/DeepAnalyze/DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:432-434 — terminal text keys on `complete.Cancelled`, so a failed file renders "Cancelled (0 done, 1 failed)" instead of "Done — 0 captioned ... (1 failed)". The UI tells the user they cancelled something they did not.

Spinner is NOT stranded (an Error event fires at line 152 and the Complete clears the card), so this is a labeling/messaging defect, not a hang or data loss — consistent with the filed "low" severity. The fix is one line in the engine and is correct.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed primary fix (set `cancelled: false` at line 167) is correct for the genuine-failure case and is the minimal change that restores the app warning toast and the correct "Done — ... (1 failed)" result line. However, it is slightly INCOMPLETE: a real user cancellation also flows through this same `Err` arm (analyze_file emits `anyhow::bail!("cancelled")`), and for that case `cancelled: true` IS the correct label. Hard-coding `false` would then mislabel an actual cancel as a failure (and surface the spurious "couldn't process this file / unsupported format" toast on a deliberate cancel). The finding already notes this in its parenthetical "optionally" — that optional refinement should be the actual fix, not optional. `cancel` (the Arc<AtomicBool> param) and `Ordering` (imported at line 5) are both in scope, so the precise fix is cheap.

confirmed.title: SEC-5 junction-traversal TOCTOU defense only checks the leaf directory because it compares a raw destination parent against a verbatim (\\?\) canonical root
confirmed.description:
`RestructureApply::apply` calls `has_reparse_point_in_chain(parent, &canonical_root)` at lines 111 and 127 with `parent = dest.parent()`, where `dest = PathBuf::from(&m.destination)` is the RAW, non-canonical destination from the IPC plan (built from the app-sent `library_root`, e.g. `D:\Library\Photos\2024`). `canonical_root` is produced by `canonicalize_safely` -> `std::fs::canonicalize`, which on Windows returns a verbatim extended-length path (e.g. `\\?\D:\Library`).

Inside `has_reparse_point_in_chain` the walk-up loop terminates on `if cur == root || !cur.starts_with(root)`. Because the raw `parent` (`D:\Library\...`) never `starts_with` the verbatim `canonical_root` (`\\?\D:\Library`), the `!cur.starts_with(root)` branch fires on the FIRST iteration. The loop checks the single leaf `parent` directory for a reparse point, then breaks — it never walks up through the intermediate ancestors toward `library_root`.

I verified this empirically with a standalone Rust probe on this Windows box: `std::fs::canonicalize` returns a `\\?\`-prefixed path, a raw child path does NOT `starts_with` that canonical root, and the exact loop body breaks after 1 step instead of the 3 needed to reach the root. The sibling restore-containment check in commands/trash.rs:54-57 does this correctly — it canonicalizes BOTH the candidate and the roots — which highlights that restructure_apply is the inconsistent site (it canonicalizes only the root).
confirmed.impact: The documented SEC-5 TOCTOU defense ("walk every ancestor of `path` up to `root` and reject if any is a reparse point") is silently reduced to checking only the immediate parent directory. The `ensure_inside_root` canonicalize check at the top of `apply` is itself TOCTOU-vulnerable to a junction/symlink planted AFTER canonicalization — which is precisely why SEC-5 exists as the defense-in-depth layer. A local attacker who plants a junction in an INTERMEDIATE pre-existing folder under `library_root` (any directory above the leaf parent but below the root) between the canonicalize and the `create_dir_all`/`MoveFileExW` can have the move resolve through it and redirect a restructure write OUTSIDE the user's chosen library root — the exact escape SEC-5 was added to eliminate. Bounded (requires local write access to a library subfolder and a race window), but it nullifies a deliberately-added security control on every real Apply.
confirmed.fix: Walk the canonical form of `parent` so both operands are the same path form. Either canonicalize `parent` before walking (mirroring trash.rs), e.g. pass `canonicalize_safely(parent)?`/`canonicalize_for_containment(parent)` into `has_reparse_point_in_chain`, or have the function canonicalize `cur`/`root` to a common normalized form (e.g. both stripped of, or both carrying, the `\\?\` prefix via `strip_extended_length`) before the `starts_with` comparison. The walk must continue up to `library_root` rather than breaking on the first form-mismatch. Add a regression test that plants a junction in an intermediate ancestor (not the leaf parent) and asserts the move is rejected.
confirmed.evidence: restructure_apply.rs:95 `let dest = PathBuf::from(&m.destination);` (raw, non-canonical); :105 `if let Some(parent) = dest.parent()`; :111/:127 `has_reparse_point_in_chain(parent, &canonical_root)`; :462-478 loop with `if cur == root || !cur.starts_with(root) { break; }`. canonical_root comes from :66 `canonicalize_safely(&self.library_root)` -> :418 `std::fs::canonicalize` (verbatim on Windows). Empirical probe output: canonical = `\\?\C:\...`, raw_child_starts_with_canonical_root = false, walkup_steps_before_break = 1 (needed >=3). Contrast with the correct sibling check at commands/trash.rs:46-57 which canonicalizes both sides.

verdict.reasoning:
I verified the finding by reading the actual code and running standalone Rust probes on this Windows box.

MECHANICS CONFIRMED (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/restructure_apply.rs):
- :95 `let dest = PathBuf::from(&m.destination);` — raw, non-canonical destination from the IPC plan.
- :105 `if let Some(parent) = dest.parent()` — `parent` is raw (e.g. `D:\Library\Photos\2024`).
- :111 / :127 call `has_reparse_point_in_chain(parent, &canonical_root)`.
- :66 `canonical_root = canonicalize_safely(&self.library_root)` → :419 `std::fs::canonicalize`, and `self.library_root` is the RAW app-sent path (commands/restructure.rs:279 `PathBuf::from(payload.library_root)` — never canonicalized at construction).
- The function (462-479) walks up with the break at :473 `if cur == root || !cur.starts_with(root) { break; }`.

EMPIRICAL PROOF:
1. `std::fs::canonicalize` returns a verbatim `\\?\C:\...` path; a raw child path does NOT `starts_with` it (Rust treats `VerbatimDisk` and `Disk` prefixes as non-matching). So `!cur.starts_with(root)` fires on iteration 1.
2. I planted a junction at an INTERMEDIATE ancestor (`root\Photos`, above the leaf parent `root\Photos\2024`) with `mklink /J` (no admin needed) pointing OUTSIDE the root, and ran the EXACT function from the source: it returned `false` — the intermediate junction is NOT detected. The walk checks only `symlink_metadata` on the leaf parent (step 1) then breaks before visiting `root\Photos` or `root`.
3. A clean tree (no junction) correctly returns `false` (no over-rejection), and a junction AT the leaf parent IS caught (because `symlink_metadata` reads the leaf on step 1 before the break) — confirming the check is reduced to leaf-only.

IMPACT: The SEC-5 TOCTOU defense-in-depth ("walk every ancestor up to root, reject if any is a reparse point") is silently degraded to checking only the immediate destination parent. A local attacker who plants a junction in a pre-existing INTERMEDIATE library subfolder (above the leaf parent, below library_root) during the window between the `ensure_inside_root` canonicalize and the `create_dir_all`/`MoveFileExW` can redirect a restructure write OUTSIDE the chosen library root — the exact escape SEC-5 exists to eliminate. Bounded (needs local write access to a library subfolder + a race), and the primary `ensure_inside_root` gate still applies, which is why this is defense-in-depth and medium, not high.

FINDING'S PROPOSED FIX IS PARTLY WRONG: the finding offers two options — (a) canonicalize `parent` before walking (mirroring trash.rs), or (b) strip the `\\?\` prefix to a common form. I empirically tested both: option (a) is BROKEN because `std::fs::canonicalize` RESOLVES the junction, yielding the junction's outside target, so the walk-up no longer starts under root and STILL returns false (bug persists). Only option (b), the form-normalization via `strip_extended_length` (which does not resolve symlinks), works — I verified it returns true for the intermediate-junction case and false for the clean tree.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's diagnosis is correct and the line numbers are exact (function 462-479, break 473, calls 111/127, raw dest 95). However the proposed fix is ambiguous and one of its two suggested options is actively wrong: canonicalizing `parent` with std::fs::canonicalize (the "mirror trash.rs" option) RESOLVES the junction itself and therefore defeats reparse detection entirely (I proved this — it still returns false for an intermediate junction). trash.rs gets away with canonicalize-both because it only does a containment `starts_with`, not a per-ancestor reparse-attribute walk; copying that pattern here would be incorrect. The correct fix is the finding's OTHER option: normalize the FORM of both operands (strip the `\\?\` prefix) without resolving the link, so `starts_with` compares like-for-like and the walk continues up to library_root. corrected_fix gives the working version.

confirmed.title: New-group folder dedup keys on the pre-sanitized name, so distinct clusters can collide into one physical folder
confirmed.description: In the new-group branch, the collision-disambiguation set `used_group_names` is keyed on the human-facing `pretty` name (inserted at line 197 and checked at lines 187/193), but the actual destination directory is `library_root.join(safe_filename_component(&pretty))` (line 216). `safe_filename_component` maps the filesystem-illegal characters `< > : " / \ | ? *` to `_`, strips trailing dots/spaces, and prefixes reserved names. Two clusters whose distinctive-tag-derived `pretty` names differ ONLY in characters that this sanitization normalizes therefore pass the `used_group_names` uniqueness check (distinct `pretty`) yet resolve to the SAME on-disk `safe` folder. The disambiguation loop (lines 187-197) was added specifically to fix issue #9 ("two clusters with identical top tags collapse into one folder"), but it operates one transformation too early and is silently bypassed for these inputs. Concrete reachable case using the code's own cited examples: cluster A top term yields pretty="16:9" -> safe="16_9"; cluster B yields pretty="16/9" -> safe="16_9" (or "dog/cat" vs "dog:cat"). Both clusters are distinct `category` values (so they render as two groups via `restructure::category_counts`, which groups by `category`), but every member ProposedMove points its `destination` at the identical `16_9` folder.
confirmed.impact: During restructure apply, files from two semantically distinct content clusters are moved into one physical folder, mixing unrelated content the butler explicitly promised to keep separate. The UI shows two distinct proposed groups (grouped by `category`/`pretty`) while the moves all land in one directory, so the on-disk result silently diverges from the previewed plan. This is exactly the #9 collision the disambiguation code exists to prevent, reachable whenever a distinctive tag contains a path-separator/illegal character (aspect-ratio tags like "16:9", compound tags like "dog/cat").
confirmed.fix: Disambiguate on the sanitized folder name, not the pretty name. Compute `let safe = crate::util::path_safety::safe_filename_component(&pretty);` first, then check/insert `safe` (not `pretty`) into `used_group_names`, regenerating `pretty`+`safe` together inside the dedup loop until `safe` is unique. Keep `pretty` only for the human-facing `category`/`reason`. (Equivalently: run the numeric-suffix / extra-term loop against the post-sanitization name so collisions are detected in the namespace that actually backs the folder.)

verdict.reasoning:
I independently traced the full data flow and confirmed the bug is real and reachable.

CODE FACTS (verified by reading the actual lines):
1. In semantic_classify (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/restructure_semantic.rs), the new-group branch checks/inserts `used_group_names` keyed on `pretty` (line 187 contains-check, line 193 contains-check in the numeric loop, line 197 insert). The on-disk folder is `library_root.join(&safe)` where `safe = crate::util::path_safety::safe_filename_component(&pretty)` (lines 216-217). The disambiguation set is therefore keyed in a DIFFERENT namespace than the one that backs the folder.

2. safe_filename_component (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/util/path_safety.rs:191-226) maps every char in `['<','>',':','"','/','\\','|','?','*']` and all control chars (<32) to `_`, lowercases the basename before a RESERVED check, strips trailing dots/spaces. So distinct `pretty` values collapse: `"16:9"`->`"16_9"` and `"16/9"`->`"16_9"`; `"dog:cat"`->`"dog_cat"` and `"dog/cat"`->`"dog_cat"`. The function's own test asserts `"Mom: Vacation"`->`"Mom_ Vacation"`, confirming the mapping.

REACHABILITY (the part I tried hardest to refute — it holds):
- `pretty` is built from tags: distinctive_terms (line 406) -> group_name_from_terms (line 441) -> title_case (line 485). title_case only upcases first letters and splits on whitespace; it does NOT strip `:` `/` `\` etc. So a tag like `16:9` survives into `pretty`.
- Tags fed to semantic_classify come from commands/restructure.rs:154: `SELECT DISTINCT file_id, tag FROM tags WHERE source IN ('auto','vlm','user')`.
- USER tags (commands/bulk.rs:123-138): inserted with only `.trim()` + non-empty filtering — NO character sanitization. A user tagging photos `16:9` vs `16/9` (or `dog:cat` vs `dog/cat`) is fully reachable and plausible.
- VLM tags (pipeline/deep_analyze.rs:335 + parse_vlm_tags at deep_analyze.rs:583): free-text model output; parse_vlm_tags strips leading list markers and surrounding quotes/dots but NOT mid-string `:` or `/`. A single-token tag like `cat/dog` or `16:9` (no internal whitespace, <=40 chars, <=2 "words") passes through verbatim.

COLLISION IS NOT SAVED DOWNSTREAM:
- category_counts (pipeline/restructure.rs:246-257) buckets by `m.category`, which is the UNSANITIZED `pretty` (passed as the category at restructure_semantic.rs:217). So the UI preview shows TWO distinct groups (`"16:9"` and `"16/9"`).
- At apply time, restructure_apply.rs uses unique_destination (line 358) which only uniquifies the LEAF FILENAME within the shared parent (bumps to ` (2)` siblings to avoid overwrite). It keeps `dest.parent()` intact, so it does NOT re-separate the two clusters — both clusters' files land in the same physical `library_root/16_9` folder.

NET: two semantically distinct clusters that the disambiguation loop (lines 187-197, added for issue #9) is supposed to keep in separate folders are silently merged into one on-disk folder whenever a distinctive tag contains an illegal/separator char that sanitization normalizes to the same string. The preview (two groups) diverges from the on-disk result (one folder). This is precisely the #9 collision the loop exists to prevent, bypassed because the loop runs one transformation too early.
verdict.fix_assessment: The prior auditor's proposed fix is directionally correct and addresses the root cause: disambiguate in the sanitized namespace, not the pretty namespace. However, the fix text is a prose description rather than exact code, and it must re-sanitize EACH candidate (the base, the `+terms[2]` variant, and each numeric-suffix variant) before checking `used_group_names`, then track the winning `safe` in the set. A naive "compute safe once, then loop on pretty" would not fix it. Below is the precise corrected implementation. Note the loop must also handle the (rarer) case where appending `terms[2]` still sanitizes to a taken `safe`, which the numeric loop already covers.
